:::: {.panel-tabset .nav-pills} ## Prison Population
::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

New Court Commitments vs. Parole Revocations

Prison populations are shaped by two primary groups: new court commitments, or individuals newly incarcerated for a criminal offense, and parole returns/revocations, those who return to prison for failing to meet the conditions of their parole. Understanding the relative proportion of these two groups is pivotal for evaluating the effectiveness of a state's criminal justice policies.



``` ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

## Parole Eligibility
::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

How is Parole Eligibility Determined?

Misdemeanants with an aggregated sentence of over 60 days and felons are eligible for parole. However, the method of determining release eligibility varies depending on whether an offender is sentenced to state prison (for a sentence of over two and a half years) or to a House of Correction (for a sentence of under two and a half years). Minimum sentences are set by the court, with some limitations. State prison sentences must be at minimum one year in length. For those sentenced to life in prison but given the possibility of parole, judges may set a minimum sentence of between 15 and 25 years. The maximum term is also set by the court, but may not exceed the longest statutory punishment for the crime of conviction. Good conduct credits earned for work or education reduce both the minimum term, (for purposes of parole eligibility calculations), and the maximum term, (for purposes of reducing the total term to be served in prison). In general, good conduct credits may reduce a sentence by up to one-third, or 10 days per month.


``` ::: ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

Parole-Eligible Prison Population

In 2020, there were 1,415 people were eligible for parole but still in prison for new crimes, with original sentence lengths ranging from 1 to 25 years. This group made up 20% of the prison population.


``` ::: ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

People who are Eligible for Parole

``` ::: ::: ::: {.panel-tabset} ### Race and Ethnicity ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

In 2020, Hispanic, any race people made up the largest proportion of those eligible for parole yet still incarcerated for a new criminal offense, comprising 37% of the parole-eligible population serving time for new crimes and with an original sentence length between 1-25 years.

``` ::: ::: ### Gender ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

In 2020, male people made up the largest proportion of those eligible for parole yet still incarcerated for a new criminal offense, comprising 95% of the parole-eligible population serving time for new crimes and with an original sentence length between 1-25 years.

``` ::: ::: ### Age ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

In 2020, people who were between the ages of 25-34 years made up the largest proportion of those eligible for parole yet still incarcerated for a new criminal offense, comprising 33% of the parole-eligible population serving time for new crimes and with an original sentence length between 1-25 years.

``` ::: ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

Sentence Lengths

In 2020, the majority of the parole-eligible prison population serving sentences for new crimes with sentence lengths ranging from 1 to 25 years were incarcerated with an original sentence length of 2-4.9 years, accounting for 42% of this group.


``` ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

Criminal Offenses

In 2020, the majority of the parole-eligible prison population serving sentences for new crimes with sentence lengths ranging from 1 to 25 years were incarcerated for offenses related to drugs, accounting for 28% of this group.


``` ::: :::

Predicted Probabilities

Predicted probabilities: Controlling for offense and sentence length, predicted probabilities of release before/at eligibility date. Something like “After controlling for differences in ages, offenses, and sentence length, 66% of White people, 40% of Black people, and 55% of Hispanic people are released before/on parole eligibility date. Similarly, 80% of women and 74% of men were released before/on parole eligibility date

``` ::: :::

:::: ::: citation-section **Notes:** NCRP data is from 2020. The parole-eligible prison population only includes people with sentence lengths ranging between 1-25 years and people incarcerated for a new crime.
